main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Quantitative framework for evaluating image originality based on model familiarity with training data","Measuring image originality via the number of tokens required for accurate reconstruction using soft prompt methods (textual inversion)","Using latent representation length as a measure of image complexity and originality","Empirical validation using both pretrained and custom-trained Stable Diffusion models","Correlation analysis between ease of concept recreation (number of tokens) and originality using DreamSim"],"problems":["Quantifying originality in images generated by text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models","Distinguishing between generic and original content generated by T2I models","Developing metrics for auditing generative models with respect to originality and copyright concerns","Addressing the challenge of measuring creativity and originality in generative models without requiring full transparency of training data"],"datasets":["Synthetic dataset of various shapes, colors, sizes, and infills (custom-created for the study)","Real-world data (not specified in detail)"],"metrics":["Number of tokens required for accurate image reconstruction","Latent representation length","DreamSim similarity score (for correlation analysis)"],"results":[{"metric":"Correlation between number of tokens and image originality (DreamSim)","value":"Positive correlation observed; more original images require more tokens for accurate reconstruction"},{"metric":"Generalization capability on synthetic data","value":"Stable diffusion models can recreate unseen elements when trained on sufficiently diverse data"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new quantitative framework for measuring image originality in T2I models based on the model's own familiarity with concepts, rather than training data transparency or memorization constraints.","Introduces the use of the number of tokens required for accurate reconstruction (via textual inversion) as a practical metric for originality.","Demonstrates that T2I models themselves can be used to discriminate between generic and original content, providing a new tool for auditing generative models.","Provides empirical evidence that embracing memorization may enable generative models to produce more innovative and diverse content."]}

methods: Quantitative framework for evaluating image originality based on model familiarity with training data
Measuring image originality via the number of tokens required for accurate reconstruction using soft prompt methods (textual inversion)
Using latent representation length as a measure of image complexity and originality
Empirical validation using both pretrained and custom-trained Stable Diffusion models
Correlation analysis between ease of concept recreation (number of tokens) and originality using DreamSim
problems: Quantifying originality in images generated by text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models
Distinguishing between generic and original content generated by T2I models
Developing metrics for auditing generative models with respect to originality and copyright concerns
Addressing the challenge of measuring creativity and originality in generative models without requiring full transparency of training data
datasets: Synthetic dataset of various shapes, colors, sizes, and infills (custom-created for the study)
metrics: Number of tokens required for accurate image reconstruction

results.metric: Correlation between number of tokens and image originality (DreamSim)
results.value: Positive correlation observed; more original images require more tokens for accurate reconstruction

results.metric: Generalization capability on synthetic data
results.value: Stable diffusion models can recreate unseen elements when trained on sufficiently diverse data

novelty_claims: Proposes a new quantitative framework for measuring image originality in T2I models based on the model's own familiarity with concepts, rather than training data transparency or memorization constraints.
Introduces the use of the number of tokens required for accurate reconstruction (via textual inversion) as a practical metric for originality.
Demonstrates that T2I models themselves can be used to discriminate between generic and original content, providing a new tool for auditing generative models.
Provides empirical evidence that embracing memorization may enable generative models to produce more innovative and diverse content.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Holistic Evaluation of Text-to-Image Models (HEIM) benchmark","Curation of 62 evaluation scenarios covering 12 aspects","Use of 25 evaluation metrics (both automated and human-rated)","Crowdsourced human evaluation in addition to automated metrics","Standardized evaluation and comparison of 26 state-of-the-art text-to-image models"],"problems":["Lack of comprehensive quantitative understanding of text-to-image model capabilities and risks","Existing benchmarks focus narrowly on text-image alignment and image quality, neglecting other important aspects","Over-reliance on automated metrics that do not capture human perception, especially for aesthetics and photorealism","Lack of standardized evaluation procedures, making model comparison difficult"],"datasets":["HEIM benchmark (62 scenarios curated for 12 aspects)","MS-COCO (used in some scenarios)","LAION aesthetics (used in some scenarios)"],"metrics":["Text-image alignment (CLIPScore, human-rated)","Image quality (FID, Inception Score, human-rated)","Aesthetics (LAION aesthetics, human-rated)","Originality (human-rated)","Reasoning (object detection, spatial reasoning, human-rated)","Knowledge (human-rated)","Bias (gender proportion, stereotype metrics, human-rated)","Toxicity (NSFW detection, human-rated)","Fairness (equivalence of alignment, human-rated)","Robustness (invariance of alignment, human-rated)","Multilinguality (invariance of alignment, human-rated)","Efficiency (inference time)"],"results":[{"metric":"No single model excels in all aspects","value":"Different models show different strengths; e.g., DALL-E 2 excels in alignment, Openjourney in aesthetics, minDALL-E and Safe Stable Diffusion in bias/toxicity mitigation"},{"metric":"Correlation between human and automated metrics","value":"Generally weak, especially for photorealism and aesthetics"},{"metric":"Performance in reasoning and multilinguality","value":"Most models perform poorly in these aspects"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of HEIM, a holistic benchmark evaluating text-to-image models across 12 diverse aspects, not just alignment and quality","Curation of 62 scenarios and 25 metrics for comprehensive evaluation","Combination of automated and crowdsourced human evaluation for more accurate assessment","Standardized, transparent, and extensible evaluation framework enabling fair comparison of 26 state-of-the-art models","Release of evaluation pipeline, code, generated images, and human evaluation results for full transparency and reproducibility"]}

methods: Holistic Evaluation of Text-to-Image Models (HEIM) benchmark
Curation of 62 evaluation scenarios covering 12 aspects
Use of 25 evaluation metrics (both automated and human-rated)
Crowdsourced human evaluation in addition to automated metrics
Standardized evaluation and comparison of 26 state-of-the-art text-to-image models
problems: Lack of comprehensive quantitative understanding of text-to-image model capabilities and risks
Existing benchmarks focus narrowly on text-image alignment and image quality, neglecting other important aspects
Over-reliance on automated metrics that do not capture human perception, especially for aesthetics and photorealism
Lack of standardized evaluation procedures, making model comparison difficult
datasets: HEIM benchmark (62 scenarios curated for 12 aspects)

results.metric: No single model excels in all aspects
results.value: Different models show different strengths; e.g., DALL-E 2 excels in alignment, Openjourney in aesthetics, minDALL-E and Safe Stable Diffusion in bias/toxicity mitigation

results.metric: Correlation between human and automated metrics
results.value: Generally weak, especially for photorealism and aesthetics

results.metric: Performance in reasoning and multilinguality
results.value: Most models perform poorly in these aspects

novelty_claims: Introduction of HEIM, a holistic benchmark evaluating text-to-image models across 12 diverse aspects, not just alignment and quality
Curation of 62 scenarios and 25 metrics for comprehensive evaluation
Combination of automated and crowdsourced human evaluation for more accurate assessment
Standardized, transparent, and extensible evaluation framework enabling fair comparison of 26 state-of-the-art models
Release of evaluation pipeline, code, generated images, and human evaluation results for full transparency and reproducibility

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Generate-and-filter pipeline for extracting memorized training images from diffusion models","New definitions for 'memorization' in image models","Two-stage data extraction attack using membership inference scoring criteria","Training hundreds of diffusion models under various settings (e.g., model accuracy, hyperparameters, augmentation, deduplication) to analyze privacy"],"problems":["Memorization and regeneration of individual training images by diffusion models","Privacy risks and data leakage in state-of-the-art image diffusion models","Effectiveness of privacy-enhancing techniques for diffusion models"],"datasets":["Stable Diffusion training set","Imagen training set","CIFAR10"],"metrics":["Number of extracted memorized images","Membership inference score","l2 distance between generated and training images","Amount of training data leaked compared to GANs"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of extracted memorized images","value":"Over a thousand from state-of-the-art models"},{"metric":"Number of near-identical replicas extracted from Stable Diffusion and Imagen","value":"Over a hundred"},{"metric":"l2 distance (example)","value":"0.031"},{"metric":"Training data leakage compared to GANs","value":"Diffusion models leak more than twice as much training data as GANs"}],"novelty_claims":["First to show that state-of-the-art diffusion models memorize and regenerate individual training examples at test time","Propose and implement new definitions for 'memorization' in image models","Devise a two-stage data extraction attack for diffusion models","Comprehensive analysis of how modeling and data decisions affect privacy in diffusion models","Demonstrate that existing privacy-enhancing techniques are insufficient for diffusion models"]}

methods: Development of DreamSim, a new perceptual similarity metric tuned to human judgments
Collection of the NIGHTS dataset using synthetic image triplets generated by diffusion models and iterative filtering
Tuning large pre-trained vision models on the NIGHTS dataset to better align with human perception
Analysis of DreamSim's sensitivity to different visual attributes (foreground, color, layout)
Evaluation of DreamSim on downstream tasks such as image retrieval and image synthesis
problems: Existing perceptual similarity metrics fail to capture mid-level and high-level similarities such as image layout, object pose, and semantic content
Current metrics are limited to low-level pixel and patch comparisons and do not align well with human visual similarity judgments
Lack of datasets that capture cognitively impenetrable, human-consistent similarity judgments over diverse image perturbations
datasets: NIGHTS (Novel Image Generations with Human-Tested Similarity) dataset (20,000 synthetic image triplets with human similarity judgments)
metrics: Accuracy in predicting human similarity judgments

results.metric: Accuracy in predicting human judgments on NIGHTS dataset

novelty_claims: Introduction of DreamSim, a perceptual similarity metric that bridges the gap between low-level and high-level image similarity and is tuned to human perception
Creation of the NIGHTS dataset, a large-scale, cognitively impenetrable set of synthetic image triplets with human similarity judgments
Demonstration that DreamSim, trained on synthetic data, generalizes well to real images and outperforms prior learned metrics and large vision models on retrieval and reconstruction tasks
Analysis showing DreamSim's focus on foreground objects and semantic content, while maintaining sensitivity to color and layout

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FT-Shield watermarking framework for text-to-image diffusion models","Novel watermark generation algorithm with a training objective incorporating fine-tuning loss of diffusion models","Mixture of Experts (MoE) based watermark detection framework"],"problems":["Unauthorized fine-tuning of text-to-image diffusion models leading to copyright infringement","Difficulty in embedding watermarks that are learned before style features during fine-tuning","Robust watermark detection across various fine-tuning methods"],"datasets":["Multiple datasets for style transfer and object transfer tasks (exact names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Watermark detection accuracy","Robustness to different fine-tuning methods","Speed of watermark acquisition during fine-tuning"],"results":[{"metric":"Watermark acquisition speed","value":"FT-Shield watermark is learned by the model at the very early stage of fine-tuning, before the style is adopted (qualitative improvement over Ma et al., 2023)"},{"metric":"Detection robustness","value":"FT-Shield detector is effective across various fine-tuning methods (DreamBooth, Textual Inversion, Text-to-Image FineTuning, LoRA)"}],"novelty_claims":["FT-Shield introduces a new watermark generation algorithm that ensures the watermark is learned by the diffusion model at the early stage of fine-tuning, before style features are acquired.","FT-Shield is the first to incorporate a Mixture of Experts (MoE) approach for robust watermark detection across different fine-tuning methods.","FT-Shield addresses the challenge of watermark feature learning order, which previous methods failed to solve, ensuring effective copyright protection against unauthorized fine-tuning."]}

methods: FT-Shield watermarking framework for text-to-image diffusion models
Novel watermark generation algorithm with a training objective incorporating fine-tuning loss of diffusion models
Mixture of Experts (MoE) based watermark detection framework
problems: Unauthorized fine-tuning of text-to-image diffusion models leading to copyright infringement
Difficulty in embedding watermarks that are learned before style features during fine-tuning
Robust watermark detection across various fine-tuning methods
datasets: Multiple datasets for style transfer and object transfer tasks (exact names not specified in the provided content)
metrics: Speed of watermark acquisition during fine-tuning

results.metric: Watermark acquisition speed
results.value: FT-Shield watermark is learned by the model at the very early stage of fine-tuning, before the style is adopted (qualitative improvement over Ma et al., 2023)

results.metric: Detection robustness
results.value: FT-Shield detector is effective across various fine-tuning methods (DreamBooth, Textual Inversion, Text-to-Image FineTuning, LoRA)

novelty_claims: FT-Shield introduces a new watermark generation algorithm that ensures the watermark is learned by the diffusion model at the early stage of fine-tuning, before style features are acquired.
FT-Shield is the first to incorporate a Mixture of Experts (MoE) approach for robust watermark detection across different fine-tuning methods.
FT-Shield addresses the challenge of watermark feature learning order, which previous methods failed to solve, ensuring effective copyright protection against unauthorized fine-tuning.

methods: InfoPrompt: an information-theoretic soft prompt tuning framework
Mutual information-based loss functions for prompt initialization and task-relevant information learning
Head loss: maximizes mutual information between prompt and language head
Representation loss: maximizes conditional mutual information between prompt tokens and feature representations
problems: Sensitivity of soft prompt tuning to prompt initialization
Inability of conventional prompt tuning methods to encode and learn sufficient task-relevant information from prompt tokens
Inefficiency and slow convergence of prompt tuning compared to conventional fine-tuning methods
datasets: Three datasets in total (only MRPC and SST-2 are named in the provided text)
metrics: Convergence speed (number of epochs to converge)
Task accuracy (implied by outperforming traditional methods)

results.metric: Conditional Mutual Information (CMI) on MRPC and SST-2
results.value: InfoPrompt achieves the highest CMI among all methods, indicating the highest relevance between prompt tokens and downstream tasks

results.metric: Convergence speed
results.value: InfoPrompt significantly accelerates convergence compared to traditional prompt tuning methods

results.metric: Task performance
results.value: InfoPrompt outperforms traditional prompt tuning methods on 6 tasks across 3 datasets

novelty_claims: Formulation of soft prompt tuning as maximizing mutual information between prompts and model parameters/representations
Development of two novel mutual information-based loss functions for prompt initialization and task-relevant information learning
InfoPrompt does not require any prior knowledge for prompt initialization
Theoretical guarantee that gradient descent can be used to train the proposed mutual information losses
Empirical demonstration that InfoPrompt accelerates convergence and improves performance over traditional prompt tuning methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-supervised Cross-view Representation Reconstruction (SCORER) network","Multi-head Token-wise Matching (MTM) for cross-view feature matching","Cross-view contrastive alignment for view-invariant representation learning","Cross-attention-based reconstruction of unchanged object representations","Cross-modal Backward Reasoning (CBR) module for improving caption informativeness","Transformer decoder for caption generation"],"problems":["Change captioning: describing the difference between a pair of similar images","Learning stable difference representations under pseudo changes caused by viewpoint changes","Resisting pseudo changes (e.g., object scale/location shifts due to viewpoint) while capturing real changes","Improving the informativeness and quality of generated change captions"],"datasets":["Four public change captioning datasets (not named explicitly in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Not specified in the provided content (likely standard captioning metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE, but not explicitly mentioned)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes SCORER, a self-supervised network for learning view-invariant image representations to reconstruct unchanged object representations and model stable difference representations under pseudo changes.","Introduces Multi-head Token-wise Matching (MTM) to fully interact feature subspaces between cross-view images, critical for view-invariant representation learning.","Designs a Cross-modal Backward Reasoning (CBR) module to enforce that generated captions are informative about the difference, by aligning a hallucinated representation with the 'after' image representation in a self-supervised manner.","Achieves state-of-the-art results on four public datasets with different change scenarios."]}

methods: Self-supervised Cross-view Representation Reconstruction (SCORER) network
Multi-head Token-wise Matching (MTM) for cross-view feature matching
Cross-view contrastive alignment for view-invariant representation learning
Cross-attention-based reconstruction of unchanged object representations
Cross-modal Backward Reasoning (CBR) module for improving caption informativeness
Transformer decoder for caption generation
problems: Change captioning: describing the difference between a pair of similar images
Learning stable difference representations under pseudo changes caused by viewpoint changes
Resisting pseudo changes (e.g., object scale/location shifts due to viewpoint) while capturing real changes
Improving the informativeness and quality of generated change captions
datasets: Four public change captioning datasets (not named explicitly in the provided content)
metrics: Not specified in the provided content (likely standard captioning metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE, but not explicitly mentioned)
novelty_claims: Proposes SCORER, a self-supervised network for learning view-invariant image representations to reconstruct unchanged object representations and model stable difference representations under pseudo changes.
Introduces Multi-head Token-wise Matching (MTM) to fully interact feature subspaces between cross-view images, critical for view-invariant representation learning.

Designs a Cross-modal Backward Reasoning (CBR) module to enforce that generated captions are informative about the difference, by aligning a hallucinated representation with the 'after' image representation in a self-supervised manner.
Achieves state-of-the-art results on four public datasets with different change scenarios.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SOS (Soft prompt attack against Open-Source LLMs): a training time attack based on soft prompt tuning, optimizing adversarial embeddings (soft prompts) and implanting them as token embeddings without modifying model weights","Backdoor attack using SOS: implanting a trigger token that causes the model to output a specific sentence or style","Adaptive backdoor attack: using SOS to make the model output in a specific style (e.g., pirate style) or subtly incorporate misinformation","Jailbreak attack using SOS: optimizing embeddings to allow the model to perform prohibited or harmful tasks when triggered","Prompt stealing attack using SOS: optimizing a prompt stealing token to extract the system prompt or other sensitive content from the model","Copyright token: a technique using SOS to mark copyrighted content and prevent models from using it"],"problems":["Security vulnerabilities in open-source LLMs due to third-party redistribution and fine-tuning/quantization","Risk of training time attacks (backdoor, jailbreak, prompt stealing) on open-source LLMs without modifying model weights","Lack of effective methods for copyright protection and fingerprinting in LLMs"],"datasets":["Alpaca dataset","RQA dataset","Alpaca-pirate (constructed for this work)","RQA-pirate (constructed for this work)","Harmful Behaviors dataset","MultiContext dataset (constructed for copyright protection evaluation)"],"metrics":["Exact match","Attack success rate","Refusal rate (for harmful questions)"],"results":[{"metric":"Exact match (Vicuna-7B on Alpaca, backdoor attack)","value":"999/1000"},{"metric":"Exact match (Mistral, prompt stealing attack)","value":"98/100"},{"metric":"Attack success rate (Llama 2, adaptive backdoor, RQA-pirate)","value":"99/100 (pirate-style responses)"},{"metric":"Refusal rate (Vicuna-7B, jailbreak attack, Harmful Behaviors dataset)","value":"4/100 (only 4 harmful questions refused)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes SOS, a novel training time attack framework for open-source LLMs that does not require clean data or modification of model weights, maintaining model utility","Demonstrates that SOS can execute backdoor, jailbreak, and prompt stealing attacks effectively with low computational cost","Introduces the copyright token, the first technique enabling model owners to protect copyrighted or private content in LLMs","Shows that SOS can be used for LLM fingerprinting, providing a new dimension for model verification"]}

methods: SOS (Soft prompt attack against Open-Source LLMs): a training time attack based on soft prompt tuning, optimizing adversarial embeddings (soft prompts) and implanting them as token embeddings without modifying model weights
Backdoor attack using SOS: implanting a trigger token that causes the model to output a specific sentence or style
Adaptive backdoor attack: using SOS to make the model output in a specific style (e.g., pirate style) or subtly incorporate misinformation
Jailbreak attack using SOS: optimizing embeddings to allow the model to perform prohibited or harmful tasks when triggered
Prompt stealing attack using SOS: optimizing a prompt stealing token to extract the system prompt or other sensitive content from the model
Copyright token: a technique using SOS to mark copyrighted content and prevent models from using it
problems: Security vulnerabilities in open-source LLMs due to third-party redistribution and fine-tuning/quantization
Risk of training time attacks (backdoor, jailbreak, prompt stealing) on open-source LLMs without modifying model weights
Lack of effective methods for copyright protection and fingerprinting in LLMs
datasets: MultiContext dataset (constructed for copyright protection evaluation)

results.metric: Exact match (Vicuna-7B on Alpaca, backdoor attack)
Exact match (Mistral, prompt stealing attack)
Attack success rate (Llama 2, adaptive backdoor, RQA-pirate)
results.value: 99/100 (pirate-style responses)

results.metric: Refusal rate (Vicuna-7B, jailbreak attack, Harmful Behaviors dataset)
results.value: 4/100 (only 4 harmful questions refused)

novelty_claims: Proposes SOS, a novel training time attack framework for open-source LLMs that does not require clean data or modification of model weights, maintaining model utility
Demonstrates that SOS can execute backdoor, jailbreak, and prompt stealing attacks effectively with low computational cost
Introduces the copyright token, the first technique enabling model owners to protect copyrighted or private content in LLMs
Shows that SOS can be used for LLM fingerprinting, providing a new dimension for model verification

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Framework inspired by cognitive psychology to probe deep generative models using controlled, low-dimensional feature spaces (e.g., shape, color, size, numerosity)","Systematic construction of training datasets with specific feature combinations to analyze model generalization and inductive bias","Empirical evaluation of deep generative models (GANs and VAEs) by measuring the properties of their learned distributions in response to controlled input distributions"],"problems":["Understanding and characterizing the inductive bias of deep generative models in high-dimensional settings","Systematic investigation of generalization patterns in deep generative models, especially regarding the generation of novel attribute combinations not present in the training data"],"datasets":["CLEVR","Colored dots dataset","MNIST"],"metrics":["Distribution of generated feature values (e.g., number of objects)","Probability assigned to novel attribute combinations","Shape of the learned distribution (e.g., log-normal)","Amount of novel combinations generated as a function of training set diversity"],"results":[{"metric":"Distribution of generated numerosity (number of objects) when trained on images with 3 objects","value":"Models (GANs and VAEs) generate 2-5 objects, with a log-normal shaped distribution"},{"metric":"Generalization to unseen modes (prototype enhancement effect)","value":"When trained on images with 2 and 4 objects, models assign higher probability to 3 objects (mean), even though 3 was not in the training set"},{"metric":"Generalization to novel feature combinations","value":"With small number of combinations in training set (e.g., 20), models memorize; with larger number (e.g., 80), models generate new combinations not in training set"},{"metric":"Consistency of generalization patterns","value":"Observed patterns are consistent across datasets (CLEVR, colored dots, MNIST), models (GAN, VAE), architectures, and hyper-parameters"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new empirical framework, inspired by cognitive psychology, to systematically probe and characterize the inductive bias and generalization behavior of deep generative models","Demonstrates, for the first time, that generalization patterns in deep generative models are consistent across datasets, models, and architectures, and that some of these patterns mirror effects observed in human cognitive psychology (e.g., prototype enhancement effect)","Provides systematic evidence of when deep generative models memorize versus generate novel attribute combinations as a function of training set diversity"]}

methods: Framework inspired by cognitive psychology to probe deep generative models using controlled, low-dimensional feature spaces (e.g., shape, color, size, numerosity)
Systematic construction of training datasets with specific feature combinations to analyze model generalization and inductive bias
Empirical evaluation of deep generative models (GANs and VAEs) by measuring the properties of their learned distributions in response to controlled input distributions
problems: Understanding and characterizing the inductive bias of deep generative models in high-dimensional settings
Systematic investigation of generalization patterns in deep generative models, especially regarding the generation of novel attribute combinations not present in the training data
metrics: Distribution of generated feature values (e.g., number of objects)
Probability assigned to novel attribute combinations
Shape of the learned distribution (e.g., log-normal)
Amount of novel combinations generated as a function of training set diversity

results.metric: Distribution of generated numerosity (number of objects) when trained on images with 3 objects
results.value: Models (GANs and VAEs) generate 2-5 objects, with a log-normal shaped distribution

results.metric: Generalization to unseen modes (prototype enhancement effect)
results.value: When trained on images with 2 and 4 objects, models assign higher probability to 3 objects (mean), even though 3 was not in the training set

results.metric: Generalization to novel feature combinations
results.value: With small number of combinations in training set (e.g., 20), models memorize; with larger number (e.g., 80), models generate new combinations not in training set

results.metric: Consistency of generalization patterns
results.value: Observed patterns are consistent across datasets (CLEVR, colored dots, MNIST), models (GAN, VAE), architectures, and hyper-parameters

novelty_claims: Proposes a new empirical framework, inspired by cognitive psychology, to systematically probe and characterize the inductive bias and generalization behavior of deep generative models

Demonstrates, for the first time, that generalization patterns in deep generative models are consistent across datasets, models, and architectures, and that some of these patterns mirror effects observed in human cognitive psychology (e.g., prototype enhancement effect)
Provides systematic evidence of when deep generative models memorize versus generate novel attribute combinations as a function of training set diversity
